Client 2: And I’m Emily. I want the plan to feel… sturdy. Like we’re not overcommitting month to month.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start high-level with cash flow, then we’ll map the accounts and the mortgage. Michael, what’s your household gross income for the year—ballpark is fine, but if you know the exact, even better.
Client 1: The exact is 187150 annually, gross.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in—housing, utilities, food, insurance, the usual stuff?
Client 2: Roughly eleven-ish. The number we use is 11200 per month.

Client 2: Yes—payment. The balance is a different number. Sorry, I always mix those up.

Client 2: Yes—payment. The balance is a different number. Sorry, I always mix those up.
Advisor: No worries at all. We’ll get the balance when we do the mortgage details. Switching over to assets—when you add up the investable accounts, what’s the total you’re thinking of as investable?
Client 1: Investable assets total 621500.
Advisor: Great. Now I’m going to break that down so I understand what’s where. Do you have a big joint taxable investment account—like a brokerage-style account—held on a retirement platform?
Client 1: Yes, joint taxable held on a retirement platform. That one is 457200.

Advisor: Okay—thanks, Michael, thanks Emily. I want to zoom out from the accounts for a minute and make sure the household basics are right.
Advisor: First, just to confirm, you two are married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And in terms of how you want to work together—are you looking for this to be through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship, or more of a brokerage-only setup?

Advisor: First, just to confirm, you two are married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And in terms of how you want to work together—are you looking for this to be through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship, or more of a brokerage-only setup?
Client 1: RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. That helps me frame what you’re expecting from us—ongoing advice versus just transactions.

Client 2: I always mix this up—are you asking what we pay or the bracket?
Advisor: Good question—yeah, the bracket band you plan around, not the exact tax bill.
Client 1: We plan around 35%.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And last one in this set: when markets get choppy, how would you describe your overall risk comfort—conservative, balanced, growth, aggressive?
Client 2: I’m more cautious than Michael, but overall we’re growth.

Advisor: Hi Michael, hi Emily—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into accounts, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Michael. We’re trying to get a clean plan—retirement timing, taxes, and just making sure we’re not leaving money on the table.

Client 1: I’m in sales.
Advisor: Okay. And income—just your base gross annual income, not counting the bonus or the business income we already listed—what’s that number for you, Michael?
Client 1: My gross annual income is 106450.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Emily, I’m not going to dig into your numbers in this exact section since we’re just cleaning up Michael’s profile, but we’ll come back to you next.

Advisor: Okay—thanks, Emily. Before we jump into positioning, I want to make sure I’m tagging you correctly in the plan. Emily, you’re Client 2, right?
Client 2: Yes—Client 2.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to round out your work profile, Emily—what would you call your occupation group in plain English? Like employee, business owner, that kind of bucket.
Client 2: Business owner.
Advisor: Got it. When you say business owner, is that your main day-to-day work identity right now, or more of a side thing?

Client 2: No. Non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Perfect. While we’re here, I’m going to do a quick sweep so I don’t miss anything—income, assets, liabilities, and protection. On income, Michael, you’ve got monthly bonus income and monthly business income coming in, right?
Client 1: Yes. Bonus paid monthly, annual amount is 70200, and business income paid monthly, annual amount is 116950.
Advisor: Great. And on assets, we already talked through the investable accounts—joint taxable on a retirement platform at 457200, your bank retirement bucket at 92200, joint bank-style holding on a retirement platform at 49400, and your private markets position at a brokerage at 22750—plus the home value we just confirmed.
Client 2: Yep, that’s the set of accounts we were talking about.
